title: Further Applications of Newton's Laws: Friction, Drag, and Elasticity
description: This pathway further explores applications of Newton's laws of motion. The pathway discusses examples of specific contact forces, such as friction, air or liquid drag, and elasticity that may affect the motion or shape. It also discusses the nature of forces on both macroscopic and microscopic levels of an object. Newton's laws are applied to describe the motion of an object and to examine relationships between contact forces and other forces exerted on an object. The examples in this pathway provide practice in using vector properties of forces and free-body diagrams to determine net force.
learning_objectives: Discuss the general characteristics of friction.
Describe the various types of friction.
Calculate the magnitudes of static and kinetic frictional forces.
Define drag force and model it mathematically.
Discuss the applications of drag force.
Define terminal velocity.
Calculate and find terminal velocity.
Identify and state hooke's law.
Explain hooke's law using graphical representation between deformation and applied force.
Discuss the three types of deformations such as changes in length, sideways shear, and changes in volume.
Describe with examples the young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus.
Calculate the change in length given mass, length, and radius.

title: Forces and Vectors
description: This pathway explores the concept of forces. The basic techniques of vector algebra are discussed, which allows for an introduction to Newton's Three Laws of Motion. These laws describe how objects react when forces are applied to them. The pathway concludes with a series of questions for students to test their understanding.
learning_objectives: Illustrate how vectors can be added together using vector diagrams.
Calculate the result of several vectors acting together.
Recall newton's three laws of motion.
Calculate the acceleration of an object when several forces are acting on it.
Identify action-rection force pairs.
Define different types of forces.

title: Motion in Two Dimensions
description: This pathway expands the concepts of motion from one dimension into two dimensions. Two key applications of this concept are the pendulum and projectile motion. The pathways concludes with questions that test understanding of the topic.
learning_objectives: Calculate the motion of objects in two dimensions.
Describe the motion of an object experiencing a constant force.
Explain why all objects fall at the same rate in the absence of air resistance.
Discuss the factors that affect the period of a pendulum.
Calculate the range of a projectile.
